one_line_takeaway: ModelShield defends LLM IP against black-box model-extraction attacks by having the victim model insert its own watermark words via a system prompt and then detecting them in imitation models with a robust top-k statistical test, matching or beating prior watermarks on detection strength (p<1e-12) while nearly eliminating the quality loss they cause.
key_finding: The self-generated watermark is reliably learned by imitation models and statistically detectable while barely touching output quality. Across 10 repeats of 4,000 samples on three backbones and two datasets, imitation models (MI) always scored significantly higher than both the base model (MO) and a legitimately fine-tuned model (ML): detailed KS-test p-values were all < 1e-12 (e.g., MISTRAL-on-HC3 vs MO = 7.12e-179), and rapid t-test p-values < 1e-9, with zero false negatives or false positives observed. Harmlessness: enabling watermarking on the victim changes QA quality negligibly (HC3 ROUGE 0.149 with vs 0.146 without; WILD 0.356 vs 0.350; PPL 9.94 vs 9.86 on HC3). Against the He[7] synonym baseline at comparable detection strength, ModelShield improves user-facing quality: ROUGE up 27.27% (HC3) and 29.08% (WILD) and PPL down 15.32%/13.80%. In the InternLM-22B white-box comparison it dominates on all three axes (p=1.74e-13, ROUGE-L 0.35, PPL 3.05) versus the logit-sine baseline Sin-signal[9] (p=7.25e-7 but ROUGE-L 0.08, PPL 298.01) and confirms parameter watermark GHZ[33] (p=0.99) and content watermarks KGW[37]/CGZ[40] (p=0.51/0.97) are essentially ineffective in the extraction setting. Robustness holds: editing attacks keep detection p<1e-18; selective training on only 10% (400 samples) still gives rapid p~1e-2 and detailed p<1e-10; clean-data dilution keeps rapid p<0.05; detection is stable as theta varies by up to ~40%. Overhead is low: watermark tokens are <2.15% of response length on average and add no significant per-token latency (min p=0.1123).
core_contribution: ModelShield is a black-box, training-free, plug-and-play watermarking method to protect the intellectual property of LLMs against model extraction (imitation) attacks. It has two original components. (1) An 'adaptive self-watermarking' mechanism: instead of the prior approach of heuristically distorting the model's token distribution (synonym swaps or logit perturbations), the victim LLM is given a system-mode prompt instructing it to autonomously insert its own watermark words into each response, so the watermark rides on the model's natural generation and the words used per query are recorded by the model owner (set WM). (2) A robust IP-infringement detection procedure built on a per-sentence 'Sentence Watermark Score' (SWS = total token length of unique recorded watermark words present in a response, normalized by response length). Detection ranks sentences by SWS, keeps the top k = max(0.01*|Y|,100), and runs a one-sided one-sample t-test against a threshold theta derived from human-text scores ('rapid' verification); when the suspect's base model is known, a two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test compares the suspect's SWS distribution against a non-watermarked fine-tuned model (ML) and the original base model (MO) ('detailed contrastive' verification). Filtering to the top-scoring subset is what gives robustness against dilution/editing. The paper also releases two self-generated-watermark datasets (HC3- and WILD-based).
threat_model: An adversary has only black-box query access to a commercial victim LLM served via API, returning output tokens only (the strictest setting; no logits, embeddings, or parameters). The adversary collects query-response pairs and fine-tunes an open-source backbone (GPT-2 Large, LLAMA2-7B, or MISTRAL-7B) into an 'imitation model' MI that approaches the victim's task performance, which is an IP-infringement / model-stealing threat. Adversaries may further try to weaken the watermark by (a) editing watermarked text (random insertion/deletion/replacement/scramble), (b) training on only a subset of watermarked data (ratio eta), (c) diluting watermarked data with clean data (ratio rho), and (d) simple prompt-injection / jailbreak instructions telling the victim not to watermark. The defender (model owner) is assumed able to trace which queries a suspect made (query history Q, a standard assumption citing OpenAI's log retention and the ByteDance account suspension), and knows its own recorded watermark sets WM and the suspect outputs WS; it does not know eta or rho. The paper explicitly scopes itself to IP-protection watermarking against output-token extraction, distinguishing it from parameter watermarking (does not transfer through outputs) and content/decoding watermarking (washes out during imitation training).
limitations: (1) Watermark capacity is bounded by the entropy/length of the generated text; for very short forced outputs the embedding-success rate collapses (Table VI: at 0-5 tokens GPT-4o 23.75%, Claude-3.5-sonnet 25.00%), though most 15+ token responses reach ~100%. (2) The whole scheme presumes the defender can trace a suspect's exact query history and possesses the recorded watermark sets WM; without this traceability the detection premise weakens (the paper leans on LMaaS log-retention policies to justify it). (3) Because embedding is prompt-based, it is in principle exposed to prompt-injection/jailbreak; the authors test only three simple hand-written attacks (success stays 91-100%) and explicitly note no complex/adaptive injection was designed against ModelShield, so adaptive-adversary robustness is untested. (4) The main head-to-head baseline in the true black-box (ChatGPT) setting is limited to He[7], because the other watermarking methods require logit or generation-process access; broader baselines only appear in the white-box InternLM-22B setting. (5) An adaptive attacker aware of the system-prompt mechanism could try to strip the watermark instruction or filter recorded watermark words, a case not deeply explored.

datasets_used: Human reference corpora for threshold calibration: TWEET, NEWS, MOVIE, FINANCE, GUANACO, SENTIMENT (plus HC3/WILD human answers)
Generalization victims sampled 800/domain from HC3 (Finance, Medicine, OpenQA, Wiki-QA)
quantitative_results: Detailed KS-test detection p-values for imitation vs MO and ML all < 1e-12 (e.g. MISTRAL/HC3 vs MO = 7.12e-179; MISTRAL/WILD vs MO = 5.53e-214); rapid t-test p < 1e-9
0 false negatives and 0 false positives observed across 10 repeats x 4000 samples
Victim QA harmlessness (Table IX): HC3 ROUGE 0.149 (w/ WM) vs 0.146 (w/o), BLEU 0.079 vs 0.075, PPL 9.94 vs 9.86; WILD ROUGE 0.356 vs 0.350, PPL 13.37 vs 12.78
vs He[7] baseline at matched ~1e-8 detection: ROUGE +27.27% (HC3) / +29.08% (WILD), PPL -15.32% (HC3) / -13.80% (WILD)
InternLM-22B victim, WILD (Table XI): Ours p=1.74e-13, ROUGE-L 0.35, PPL 3.05; Sin-signal[9] p=7.25e-7, ROUGE-L 0.08, PPL 298.01; GHZ[33] p=0.99; KGW[37] p=0.51; CGZ[40] p=0.97
Watermark token percentage < 2.15% of response length on average; per-token latency two-tailed t-test min p=0.1123 (no significant slowdown)
Embedding success ~100% across GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Claude-3.5-sonnet, GLM-4-plus, Qwen-plus, DeepSeek-v2 (GPT-3.5-turbo-0125 99.0-99.5%)
Robustness: editing attacks detection p<1e-18; selective training 10% (400 samples) rapid p~1e-2 / detailed p<1e-10; clean-data dilution rapid p<0.05; detection stable across theta +/-40%
Short-output degradation (Table VI, 0-5 tokens): GPT-4o 23.75%, Claude-3.5-sonnet 25.00%, GPT-4 57.50%, GPT-4o-mini 73.75%
Threshold theta=0.11, top-k = max(0.01*|Y|,100), significance alpha=0.05
baselines_compared: He[7] - black-box lexical/synonym post-processing watermark (primary black-box baseline)
GINSEW / Sin-signal[9] - white-box logit sine-signal IP watermark
